content: I'm trying to bisect clang. What options do I pass to cmake to get a clang that works with linux and bindgen?

This seems to do it:

cmake -G Ninja -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release  -DLLVM_ENABLE_PROJECTS="clang;clang-tools-extra;lld" -DLLVM_ENABLE_RUNTIMES="compiler-rt;libunwind" -DLLVM_ENABLE_WARNINGS=OFF ../llvm && ninja
Can we make changes to LIBCLANG_PATH result in bindgen stuff getting rebuilt?

Alice Ryhl said:

Can we make changes to LIBCLANG_PATH result in bindgen stuff getting rebuilt?

You mean you want to rebuild bindgen with a different clang?
No. If you swap out libclang.so, which bindgen uses, you should regenerate bindgen stuff.
Since changing libclang could change the bindgen output.
I don't think the kernel uses LIBCLANG_PATH anywhere other than in bindgen.
Oh, you mean rust/bindings/* depends on $LIBCLANG_PATH in Makefiles?
Not sure whether that's easy to do in Makefiles though.
Can't we do something like commit ac3e972629a6 ("kbuild: rust: rebuild if the version text changes")?

I'm getting warnings like this one, so we clearly already check the version number.

***
*** libclang (used by the Rust bindings generator 'bindgen')
*** version does not match Clang's. This may be a problem.
***   libclang version: 18.0.1
***   Clang version:    19.0.1
***
***
*** Please see Documentation/rust/quick-start.rst for details
*** on how to set up the Rust support.
***
but I guess we can add the clang version information at the first line of bindings .rs files and have a script checking whether that version matches the output of indgen --version --verbose.

Alice Ryhl said:

Can't we do something like commit ac3e972629a6 ("kbuild: rust: rebuild if the version text changes")?

But LIBCLANG_PATH is not a kconfig, or you're suggesting we make it a kconfig?
Anyway, my Makefile knowledge is quite limited, maybe Miguel knows better.

No I'm not suggesting we make LIBCLANG_PATH a kconfig. I'm suggesting we do something to have bindgen output get regenerated if you change the libclang.so file being used. I don't really know how that works in details.

Hmm... there would be several ways to go about it. Tracking the file itself would be something that would need to be discussed more deeply. Tracking just the version is not enough for bisecting.

What I would do is track the version text instead, since at least for my libclang (well, Clang through bindgen) it shows the commit hash, so that should be fine for bisecting. For that I would need to send another one to switch to --verbose first, which I did and is straightforward, but I have to update the tests which is a bit more involved.
Reason I suggested is that my first bisection attempt got messed up due to this :sweat_smile:

The bisection terminated on this commit: https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/commit/2e770edd8ce13f48402f1d93e5fb982d8a2ebe64
It seems there's a related fix in bindgen: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/pull/2824
How do we handle bindgen bugs? The tracepoint stuff will break if you use an unfixed bindgen together with a clang that has that bug.
We have a min version for bindgen. To fix I guess you send a patch that bumps that minimum.
Do we really want to unconditionally bump it? The fix was released two weeks ago.
I guess I can add a Kconfig requirement that if you use a new clang, you must also use a new bindgen.
Not sure how that will fly with min-tool-version.sh though?
We already have a fair number of version requirements in Kconfig for cases where compilers are buggy.
Or missing flags.
Ok.
I guess the build will just fail if version requirements are not satisfied.
I could also add a warning saying that this combination may not work, like we already have for things like clang and libclang.so being mismatched.
I would rather break the build if it is buggy. At least require people to set CONFIG_I_KNOW_THIS_IS_STUPID or something to that effect to override.
Is it only tracepoints that break?
That's the only failure I've observed.
I guess they do complicated header magic that happens to interact badly with the clang change. I don't understand the mechanism, though.

I guess another option is to add a clause like this one:
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/c1e939a21eb111a6d6067b38e8e04b8809b64c4e/scripts/rust_is_available.sh#L166-L175
So if tracepoints are enabled, we also need either bindgen >= 0.70.0 or libclang < 19.1.0, is that correct?
Yeah
Though other headers added in the future could also trip it. I don't understand it well enough to rule that out.
Yeah, I understand
I guess distribution may patch this too
Fedora 41 has now LLVM 19.1 and bindgen 0.69
What do you prefer that I do? I can submit a clause to emit a warning and include it in the tracepoint series. Or I can send it as a fix?

I am thinking... I think the warning is a simple way out, but we cannot make it an error there [*] since it works sometimes (@Andreas Hindborg do you mean breaking the build right there?)

[*] but we would likely need a build test (I think we can copy the one from the test)
I mean, it's probably fine to just warn and explain the cause. Then it can fail later on in the build when you try to use tracepoints if the bug is actually triggered.
Yeah, it is best to have a warning, because we don't know if it will actually fail
Do you want a fix or together with tracepoint series?

Miguel Ojeda said:

I am thinking... I think the warning is a simple way out, but we cannot make it an error there [*] since it works sometimes (Andreas Hindborg do you mean breaking the build right there?)

[*] but we would likely need a build test (I think we can copy the one from the test)

Yes, if we have combination of config options that is known to miscompile, I would just bug out when we detect it.
The thing is, we don't have the config at that point, and we may be missing other cases.
So the build may work, or not, thus failing the build right there means we will prevent builds that are just fine, including building in Fedora right now.
Alice observed the error for something tracepoints, right? So if config_tracepoints + config_rust + (bad_clang_bindgen combo) -> bug
The config is not available at that point
To do what you say, we need to do it in Kconfig instead
Yes
I think that is reasonable ?
Distros might backport the fix
We cannot do a version check (only), we would need a build test (but this applies to either approach)
I don't actually have a small repro for this.
So distros might have clang reporting version a.b.c that should not have fix but then they patch it and it will report a.b.c-distro1 and it will have the fix?
Yes, that I why I do a build test for the other bug
:scream:

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't actually have a small repro for this.

Then no build test for the moment, we can add it later.

As for fix or not, I am thinking -- on one side it is not a fix since there is nothing that we know is broken, and I don't see tracepoints yet in -next. But I guess it might be best to have it early, since other things could, in principle, be broken anyway.
So, Andreas: without the build test, we cannot really do the Kconfig approach, unless we are OK blocking potentially OK combinations.
(since we would only need to rely on version checks, which may or may not be accurate)
Having said that, we do have a repro within the kernel, we could reduce that I guess.

Miguel Ojeda said:

So, Andreas: without the build test, we cannot really do the Kconfig approach, unless we are OK blocking potentially OK combinations.

The test would have false positives in case of patched toolchains. I think that is ok?
I mean, it means users cannot build the kernel
even when it would have worked
Only if they enable tracepoints and have a know bad compiler
Basically everyone enables tracepoints.
So e.g. Linus will not be able to build right now anymore
(I mean, with Rust enabled)
But will Linus compiler be patched?
I don't know, I need to ask Fedora
Or will he build a broken kernel?
Would it be an option to disable tracepoints for rust only, if toolchain does not work out?

IIUC, right now, in Fedora, if we have the warning approach, you would get the warning, and then the build will fail.

With the Kconfig approach, you would not be able to build with tracepoints or with Rust.

Am I missing anything?

Later on, if Fedora patches bindgen, then with the warning approach, you would still get the warning, but it would work.

With the Kconfig approach, nothing would change, i.e. you would still need to build without tracepoints or without Rust.

That is, assuming we don't have a build test.

If we do have a build test, then you would not get the warning anymore. And in the Kconfig approach, you would be able to build.
I think it is best to not rush and try to get a build test, since there is nothing broken right now in mainline.
I will try to reduce it.
OK, I might have misunderstood then.l I thought the bad toolchain combo would produce a bad build, not fail to build
Ah, no, it just causes bindgen to completely fail to emit some things.
Right, then everything makes a lot more sense

I was understanding it is a build error -- if it is a runtime error, then I would definitely agree. Even if it is a build error, given how recent the versions are, I am undecided if we should take the hit and do the Kconfig approach.
